titleEnglish: Arjuna Vishada Yoga - The Yoga of Arjuna's Despondency
description: Bhagavad Gita Chapter 1, Arjuna Vishada Yoga, sets the stage for the entire Gita dialogue on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. King Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya about the events on the sacred field where the Kauravas and Pandavas have assembled for war. Sanjaya describes Duryodhana approaching Dronacharya and listing the mighty warriors on both sides. As conches blow and the battle is about to begin, Arjuna asks Krishna to drive his chariot between the two armies. Seeing his grandfathers, teachers, uncles, brothers, sons, and friends arrayed for battle, Arjuna is overcome with deep compassion and sorrow. His limbs tremble, his mouth dries up, and the Gandiva bow slips from his hands. He argues that killing his own kinsmen for a kingdom would be a great sin, leading to the destruction of family dharma and social order. Overwhelmed by grief and moral confusion, Arjuna casts aside his bow and arrows and sinks into the seat of his chariot, setting the stage for Krishna's immortal teachings.

verses.speaker: Dhritarashtra
verses.devanagari:
धर्मक्षेत्रे कुरुक्षेत्रे समवेता युयुत्सवः।
मामकाः पाण्डवाश्चैव किमकुर्वत सञ्जय॥१॥
verses.transliteration:
Dharmakshetre Kurukshetre Samaveta Yuyutsavah.
Mamakah Pandavash-Chaiva Kim-Akurvata Sanjaya.||1||

wordByWord.meaning: in the field of the Kurus

verses.hindiTranslation: धृतराष्ट्र ने कहा — हे संजय! धर्मभूमि कुरुक्षेत्र में एकत्रित, युद्ध की इच्छा वाले मेरे और पाण्डु के पुत्रों ने क्या किया?
verses.englishTranslation: Dhritarashtra said: O Sanjaya, assembled on the holy field of Kurukshetra, eager to fight, what did my sons and the sons of Pandu do?
verses.commentary: This opening verse sets the entire tone of the Gita. Dhritarashtra's use of 'mamakah' (my sons) versus 'Pandavah' (sons of Pandu) reveals his deep attachment and partiality. The word 'Dharmakshetra' is significant — the field of righteousness will ultimately favor dharma over adharma.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.devanagari:
दृष्ट्वा तु पाण्डवानीकं व्यूढं दुर्योधनस्तदा।
आचार्यमुपसङ्गम्य राजा वचनमब्रवीत्॥२॥
verses.transliteration:
Drishtva Tu Pandavanikam Vyudham Duryodhanas-Tada.
Acharyam-Upasangamya Raja Vachanam-Abravit.||2||
verses.hindiTranslation: संजय ने कहा — पाण्डवों की सेना को व्यूहाकार में खड़ी देखकर राजा दुर्योधन ने तब आचार्य द्रोणाचार्य के पास जाकर यह वचन कहे।
verses.englishTranslation: Sanjaya said: Having seen the army of the Pandavas drawn up in battle formation, King Duryodhana then approached his teacher Drona and spoke these words.
verses.commentary: Duryodhana's anxiety is revealed through his immediate approach to Drona. Rather than consulting Bhishma, the commander-in-chief, he goes to Drona — subtly reminding him that Drupada's son Dhrishtadyumna, Drona's sworn enemy, leads the opposing army. This is a calculated political move disguised as respect.

verses.speaker: Duryodhana
verses.devanagari:
पश्यैतां पाण्डुपुत्राणामाचार्य महतीं चमूम्।
व्यूढां द्रुपदपुत्रेण तव शिष्येण धीमता॥३॥
verses.transliteration:
Pashyai-Tam Pandu-Putranam-Acharya Mahatim Chamum.
Vyudham Drupada-Putrena Tava Shishyena Dhimata.||3||
verses.hindiTranslation: दुर्योधन ने कहा — हे आचार्य! पाण्डु-पुत्रों की इस विशाल सेना को देखिए, जिसे आपके बुद्धिमान शिष्य द्रुपद-पुत्र धृष्टद्युम्न ने व्यूह-रचना में खड़ा किया है।
verses.englishTranslation: Duryodhana said: O teacher, behold this mighty army of the sons of Pandu, arrayed in battle formation by your intelligent disciple, the son of Drupada.
verses.commentary: Duryodhana cunningly reminds Drona that the opposing army is led by Dhrishtadyumna — Drona's own student and the son of Drupada, who was born specifically to kill Drona. By calling him 'your disciple,' Duryodhana provokes Drona's fighting spirit through a mix of flattery and implied reproach.

verses.speaker: Duryodhana
verses.devanagari:
अत्र शूरा महेष्वासा भीमार्जुनसमा युधि।
युयुधानो विराटश्च द्रुपदश्च महारथः॥४॥
verses.transliteration:
Atra Shura Maheshvasa Bhimarjuna-Sama Yudhi.
Yuyudhano Viratas-Cha Drupadas-Cha Maharathah.||4||
verses.hindiTranslation: इस सेना में भीम और अर्जुन के समान युद्ध करने वाले अनेक शूरवीर और महान धनुर्धर हैं — युयुधान (सात्यकि), विराट और महारथी द्रुपद।
verses.englishTranslation: Here in this army are many heroic bowmen equal in battle to Bhima and Arjuna — Yuyudhana (Satyaki), Virata, and the great warrior Drupada.
verses.commentary: Duryodhana begins enumerating the Pandava warriors to stir Drona's concern. By comparing them to Bhima and Arjuna, he emphasizes the threat they pose. This cataloguing of warriors reveals Duryodhana's own anxiety masked as strategic assessment.

verses.speaker: Duryodhana
verses.devanagari:
धृष्टकेतुश्चेकितानः काशिराजश्च वीर्यवान्।
पुरुजित्कुन्तिभोजश्च शैब्यश्च नरपुङ्गवः॥५॥
verses.transliteration:
Dhrishtaketush-Chekitanah Kashirajas-Cha Viryavan.
Purujit-Kuntibhojas-Cha Shaibyas-Cha Nara-Pungavah.||5||
verses.hindiTranslation: धृष्टकेतु, चेकितान, पराक्रमी काशिराज, पुरुजित, कुन्तिभोज और नरश्रेष्ठ शैब्य भी उनकी सेना में हैं।
verses.englishTranslation: Dhrishtaketu, Chekitana, the valiant King of Kashi, Purujit, Kuntibhoja, and Shaibya — the best among men — are also present.
verses.commentary: Duryodhana continues listing Pandava allies, each a powerful king in his own right. This enumeration serves to impress upon Drona the formidable nature of the opposition, while simultaneously revealing Duryodhana's own inner nervousness.

verses.speaker: Duryodhana
verses.devanagari:
युधामन्युश्च विक्रान्त उत्तमौजाश्च वीर्यवान्।
सौभद्रो द्रौपदेयाश्च सर्व एव महारथाः॥६॥
verses.transliteration:
Yudhamanyu-Sh-Cha Vikranta Uttamaujas-Cha Viryavan.
Saubhadro Draupadeyash-Cha Sarva Eva Maharathah.||6||
verses.hindiTranslation: पराक्रमी युधामन्यु, वीर उत्तमौजा, सुभद्रा-पुत्र अभिमन्यु और द्रौपदी के पाँचों पुत्र — ये सभी महारथी हैं।
verses.englishTranslation: The mighty Yudhamanyu, the valiant Uttamaujas, the son of Subhadra (Abhimanyu), and the sons of Draupadi — all of them are great chariot warriors.
verses.commentary: Duryodhana mentions Abhimanyu and the sons of Draupadi — young warriors of the next generation. The mention of so many maharathis (great warriors capable of fighting ten thousand soldiers simultaneously) shows the depth of the Pandava army's strength.

verses.speaker: Duryodhana
verses.devanagari:
अस्माकं तु विशिष्टा ये तान्निबोध द्विजोत्तम।
नायका मम सैन्यस्य संज्ञार्थं तान्ब्रवीमि ते॥७॥
verses.transliteration:
Asmakam Tu Vishishta Ye Tan-Nibodha Dvijottama.
Nayaka Mama Sainyasya Samjnartham Tan-Bravimi Te.||7||
verses.hindiTranslation: हे द्विजश्रेष्ठ! अब हमारी ओर के भी जो विशिष्ट योद्धा हैं, उन्हें भी जानिए। आपकी जानकारी के लिए मैं अपनी सेना के नायकों के नाम बताता हूँ।
verses.englishTranslation: But know also, O best of Brahmanas, the distinguished warriors on our side. For your information, I name the leaders of my army.
verses.commentary: After enumerating the Pandava warriors, Duryodhana now turns to his own side. Addressing Drona as 'Dvijottama' (best of the twice-born) is both respectful and strategic — it reminds Drona of his Brahmin duty to fight for his patron.

verses.speaker: Duryodhana
verses.devanagari:
भवान्भीष्मश्च कर्णश्च कृपश्च समितिञ्जयः।
अश्वत्थामा विकर्णश्च सौमदत्तिस्तथैव च॥८॥
verses.transliteration:
Bhavan-Bhishmas-Cha Karnas-Cha Kripas-Cha Samitinjayah.
Ashvatthama Vikarnas-Cha Saumadattis-Tathaiva Cha.||8||
verses.hindiTranslation: आप स्वयं (द्रोणाचार्य), भीष्म, कर्ण, युद्ध में सदा विजयी कृपाचार्य, अश्वत्थामा, विकर्ण और सोमदत्त-पुत्र भूरिश्रवा — ये सब हमारी ओर हैं।
verses.englishTranslation: Yourself (Drona), Bhishma, Karna, the ever-victorious Kripa, Ashvatthama, Vikarna, and Bhurishrava, the son of Somadatta — all are on our side.
verses.commentary: Duryodhana places Drona first in his list, a diplomatic move. Notably, he names Karna alongside Bhishma — two warriors who had deep mutual rivalry. This list of legendary warriors is meant to reassure both Drona and himself of their army's invincibility.

verses.speaker: Duryodhana
verses.devanagari:
अन्ये च बहवः शूरा मदर्थे त्यक्तजीविताः।
नानाशस्त्रप्रहरणाः सर्वे युद्धविशारदाः॥९॥
verses.transliteration:
Anye Cha Bahavah Shura Mad-Arthe Tyakta-Jivitah.
Nana-Shastra-Praharanah Sarve Yuddha-Visharadah.||9||

wordByWord.meaning: ready to give up their lives

verses.hindiTranslation: और भी बहुत से शूरवीर हैं जो मेरे लिए अपने प्राणों को त्यागने के लिए तैयार हैं। वे सब विभिन्न शस्त्रों से सुसज्जित और युद्धकला में निपुण हैं।
verses.englishTranslation: And many other heroes are ready to lay down their lives for my sake. They are all armed with various weapons and are skilled in the art of warfare.
verses.commentary: Duryodhana's claim that warriors are ready to die 'for my sake' reveals his ego and possessiveness. He sees the war as his personal cause rather than a matter of dharma. This self-centered view contrasts sharply with the Pandavas' fight for righteous justice.

verses.speaker: Duryodhana
verses.devanagari:
अपर्याप्तं तदस्माकं बलं भीष्माभिरक्षितम्।
पर्याप्तं त्विदमेतेषां बलं भीमाभिरक्षितम्॥१०॥
verses.transliteration:
Aparyaptam Tad-Asmakam Balam Bhishma-Abhirakshitam.
Paryaptam Tv-Idam-Etesham Balam Bhima-Abhirakshitam.||10||
verses.hindiTranslation: भीष्म द्वारा रक्षित हमारी सेना अपार है, जबकि भीम द्वारा रक्षित इनकी सेना सीमित है।
verses.englishTranslation: Our army, protected by Bhishma, is unlimited in strength, while their army, protected by Bhima, is limited.
verses.commentary: This verse has a famous double meaning. 'Aparyaptam' can mean both 'unlimited' and 'insufficient.' Some commentators interpret Duryodhana as inadvertently expressing his hidden fear — that his army under Bhishma is actually insufficient against the Pandava forces led by Bhima.

verses.speaker: Duryodhana
verses.devanagari:
अयनेषु च सर्वेषु यथाभागमवस्थिताः।
भीष्ममेवाभिरक्षन्तु भवन्तः सर्व एव हि॥११॥
verses.transliteration:
Ayaneshu Cha Sarveshu Yatha-Bhagam-Avasthitah.
Bhishmam-Eva-Abhirakshanthu Bhavantah Sarva Eva Hi.||11||
verses.hindiTranslation: इसलिए आप सब अपने-अपने स्थानों पर स्थित रहते हुए, सभी मोर्चों से निश्चित रूप से पितामह भीष्म की ही रक्षा करें।
verses.englishTranslation: Therefore, all of you, stationed at your respective positions in the formation, must protect Bhishma from all sides.
verses.commentary: Duryodhana's instruction reveals his strategic concern — Bhishma is the linchpin of the Kaurava army. If Bhishma falls, the entire army's morale would collapse. This also subtly hints at Duryodhana's distrust, as Bhishma had mixed loyalties due to his affection for the Pandavas.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.devanagari:
तस्य सञ्जनयन्हर्षं कुरुवृद्धः पितामहः।
सिंहनादं विनद्योच्चैः शङ्खं दध्मौ प्रतापवान्॥१२॥
verses.transliteration:
Tasya Sanjanayan Harsham Kuru-Vriddhah Pitamahah.
Simha-Nadam Vinadyo-Chchaih Shankham Dadhmau Pratapavan.||12||
verses.hindiTranslation: तब कुरु-वंश के वृद्ध, प्रतापी पितामह भीष्म ने दुर्योधन के हृदय में हर्ष उत्पन्न करते हुए उच्च स्वर से सिंहनाद किया और शङ्ख बजाया।
verses.englishTranslation: Then the valiant grandfather Bhishma, the oldest of the Kuru dynasty, roared loudly like a lion and blew his conch, filling Duryodhana's heart with joy.
verses.commentary: Bhishma responds not with words but with action — a thunderous lion's roar and the blast of his conch. This was meant to bolster Duryodhana's confidence. The patriarch understands the young king's anxiety and reassures him with this warrior's greeting.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.devanagari:
ततः शङ्खाश्च भेर्यश्च पणवानकगोमुखाः।
सहसैवाभ्यहन्यन्त स शब्दस्तुमुलोऽभवत्॥१३॥
verses.transliteration:
Tatah Shankhash-Cha Bheryas-Cha Panavana-Ka-Gomukhah.
Sahasai-Va-Abhyahanyanta Sa Shabdas-Tumulo'bhavat.||13||
verses.hindiTranslation: उसके बाद शङ्ख, नगाड़े, ढोल, मृदंग और गोमुख आदि वाद्य एक साथ बज उठे। वह ध्वनि अत्यन्त भयंकर थी।
verses.englishTranslation: Then conches, kettledrums, tabors, drums, and cow-horns all sounded together at once. The combined noise was tumultuous.
verses.commentary: The sudden burst of war instruments on the Kaurava side marks the escalation of tension. The tumultuous sound symbolizes the chaos of war that is about to engulf both armies. This contrasts with the more disciplined and divine conch-blowing by the Pandava side that follows.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.devanagari:
ततः श्वेतैर्हयैर्युक्ते महति स्यन्दने स्थितौ।
माधवः पाण्डवश्चैव दिव्यौ शङ्खौ प्रदध्मतुः॥१४॥
verses.transliteration:
Tatah Shvetair-Hayair-Yukte Mahati Syandane Sthitau.
Madhavah Pandavash-Chaiva Divyau Shankhau Pradadhmatuh.||14||
verses.hindiTranslation: तत्पश्चात् श्वेत अश्वों से युक्त विशाल रथ में बैठे श्रीकृष्ण (माधव) और अर्जुन (पाण्डव) ने भी अपने-अपने दिव्य शङ्ख बजाए।
verses.englishTranslation: Then, seated in a great chariot yoked with white horses, Madhava (Krishna) and Pandava (Arjuna) blew their divine conch shells.
verses.commentary: The Pandava response is majestic — Krishna and Arjuna seated on a divine chariot drawn by white horses. The whiteness symbolizes purity and dharma. Their divine conches represent the call of righteousness that will ultimately prevail over the forces of adharma.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.devanagari:
पाञ्चजन्यं हृषीकेशो देवदत्तं धनञ्जयः।
पौण्ड्रं दध्मौ महाशङ्खं भीमकर्मा वृकोदरः॥१५॥
verses.transliteration:
Panchajanyam Hrishikesho Devadattam Dhananjayah.
Paundram Dadhmau Maha-Shankham Bhima-Karma Vrikodarah.||15||
verses.hindiTranslation: हृषीकेश श्रीकृष्ण ने पाञ्चजन्य, अर्जुन ने देवदत्त, और भयंकर कर्म करने वाले भीम ने पौण्ड्र नामक महाशङ्ख बजाया।
verses.englishTranslation: Hrishikesha (Krishna) blew the Panchajanya conch, Dhananjaya (Arjuna) blew the Devadatta, and Bhima of terrible deeds blew the great conch Paundra.
verses.commentary: Each conch has a name and significance. Panchajanya was obtained by Krishna from the demon Panchajana. Devadatta was a divine gift to Arjuna. The naming of these conches elevates the Pandava warriors from mere mortals to figures of cosmic destiny.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.devanagari:
अनन्तविजयं राजा कुन्तीपुत्रो युधिष्ठिरः।
नकुलः सहदेवश्च सुघोषमणिपुष्पकौ॥१६॥
verses.transliteration:
Ananta-Vijayam Raja Kunti-Putro Yudhishthirah.
Nakulah Sahadevas-Cha Sughosha-Manipushpakau.||16||
verses.hindiTranslation: कुन्तीपुत्र राजा युधिष्ठिर ने अनन्तविजय, नकुल ने सुघोष और सहदेव ने मणिपुष्पक नामक शङ्ख बजाए।
verses.englishTranslation: King Yudhishthira, the son of Kunti, blew the Anantavijaya conch; Nakula and Sahadeva blew the Sughosha and Manipushpaka conches.
verses.commentary: The names of the conches are symbolic. Anantavijaya means 'endless victory' — fitting for Yudhishthira, the embodiment of dharma. Sughosha means 'sweet-sounding' and Manipushpaka means 'jewel-flowered,' reflecting the gentle yet powerful nature of the twin brothers.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.devanagari:
काश्यश्च परमेष्वासः शिखण्डी च महारथः।
धृष्टद्युम्नो विराटश्च सात्यकिश्चापराजितः॥१७॥
verses.transliteration:
Kashyas-Cha Parameshvasah Shikhandi Cha Maharathah.
Dhrishtadyumno Viratas-Cha Satyakis-Cha-Aparajitah.||17||
verses.hindiTranslation: श्रेष्ठ धनुर्धर काशिराज, महारथी शिखण्डी, धृष्टद्युम्न, विराट और अजेय सात्यकि ने भी अपने शङ्ख बजाए।
verses.englishTranslation: The King of Kashi — a supreme archer, the great warrior Shikhandi, Dhrishtadyumna, Virata, and the unconquered Satyaki also blew their conches.
verses.commentary: Each of these warriors played a decisive role in the war. Shikhandi was the instrument of Bhishma's fall, Dhrishtadyumna killed Drona, and Satyaki remained undefeated throughout. Their conch-blowing signals the united strength of the Pandava alliance.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.devanagari:
द्रुपदो द्रौपदेयाश्च सर्वशः पृथिवीपते।
सौभद्रश्च महाबाहुः शङ्खान्दध्मुः पृथक्पृथक्॥१८॥
verses.transliteration:
Drupado Draupadeyash-Cha Sarvashah Prithivipate.
Saubhadras-Cha Maha-Bahuh Shankhan-Dadhmuh Prithak-Prithak.||18||
verses.hindiTranslation: हे पृथिवीपते! द्रुपद, द्रौपदी के पाँचों पुत्र और महाबाहु सुभद्रा-पुत्र अभिमन्यु ने भी अलग-अलग अपने शङ्ख बजाए।
verses.englishTranslation: O Lord of the earth, Drupada, the sons of Draupadi, and the mighty-armed Abhimanyu, son of Subhadra, all blew their respective conches separately.
verses.commentary: Sanjaya addresses Dhritarashtra as 'Prithivipate' (lord of the earth) — a title that reminds the blind king of his responsibility. The individual blowing of conches by each warrior demonstrates the Pandava army's organized discipline, contrasting with the chaotic combined noise from the Kaurava side.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.devanagari:
स घोषो धार्तराष्ट्राणां हृदयानि व्यदारयत्।
नभश्च पृथिवीं चैव तुमुलो व्यनुनादयन्॥१९॥
verses.transliteration:
Sa Ghosho Dhartarashtranam Hridayani Vyadarayat.
Nabhas-Cha Prithivim Chaiva Tumulo Vyanunadayan.||19||
verses.hindiTranslation: उस भयंकर ध्वनि ने आकाश और पृथ्वी को गुंजाते हुए धृतराष्ट्र के पुत्रों के हृदयों को विदीर्ण कर दिया।
verses.englishTranslation: That tumultuous sound, reverberating through the sky and the earth, pierced the hearts of Dhritarashtra's sons.
verses.commentary: The collective sound of the Pandava conches had a devastating psychological effect on the Kauravas. This is symbolic — the call of dharma (righteousness) strikes fear in the hearts of those who stand on the side of adharma. The cosmic scale of the sound foreshadows the cosmic significance of the war.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.devanagari:
अथ व्यवस्थितान्दृष्ट्वा धार्तराष्ट्रान्कपिध्वजः।
प्रवृत्ते शस्त्रसम्पाते धनुरुद्यम्य पाण्डवः॥२०॥
verses.transliteration:
Atha Vyavasthitan Drishtva Dhartarashtran Kapi-Dhvajah.
Pravritte Shastra-Sampate Dhanur-Udyamya Pandavah.||20||

wordByWord.meaning: one with Hanuman on his flag (Arjuna)

verses.hindiTranslation: तत्पश्चात् जब शस्त्रों का प्रहार होने वाला था, तब हनुमान-ध्वज वाले अर्जुन ने धृतराष्ट्र के पुत्रों को व्यवस्थित देखकर अपना धनुष उठाया।
verses.englishTranslation: Then, seeing the sons of Dhritarashtra arrayed for battle, as the clash of weapons was about to begin, Arjuna — whose flag bore the emblem of Hanuman — raised his bow.
verses.commentary: The epithet 'Kapi-dhvajah' (Hanuman on his flag) is deeply significant. Hanuman represents devotion, strength, and selfless service — qualities that connect the Ramayana to the Mahabharata. Arjuna initially appears ready to fight, making his subsequent breakdown all the more dramatic.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
हृषीकेशं तदा वाक्यमिदमाह महीपते।
सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये रथं स्थापय मेऽच्युत॥२१॥
verses.transliteration:
Hrishikesham Tada Vakyam-Idam-Aha Mahipate.
Senayor-Ubhayor-Madhye Ratham Sthapaya Me'chyuta.||21||
verses.hindiTranslation: अर्जुन ने कहा — हे अच्युत! मेरे रथ को दोनों सेनाओं के बीच में खड़ा कीजिए।
verses.englishTranslation: Arjuna said: O infallible one (Achyuta), place my chariot between the two armies.
verses.commentary: Arjuna addresses Krishna as 'Achyuta' — the infallible one who never falls from His position. This request to stand between both armies seems like a warrior's assessment of the enemy, but it becomes the catalyst for his emotional and spiritual crisis.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
यावदेतान्निरीक्षेऽहं योद्धुकामानवस्थितान्।
कैर्मया सह योद्धव्यमस्मिन्रणसमुद्यमे॥२२॥
verses.transliteration:
Yavad-Etan-Niriksha'ham Yoddhu-Kaman-Avasthitan.
Kair-Maya Saha Yoddhavyam-Asmin Rana-Samudyame.||22||
verses.hindiTranslation: ताकि मैं इन युद्ध की इच्छा रखने वाले योद्धाओं को देख सकूँ कि इस युद्ध में मुझे किन-किन से लड़ना है।
verses.englishTranslation: So that I may observe those who stand here eager to fight, and know with whom I must contend in this great battle.
verses.commentary: Arjuna's request seems military in nature — a commander wanting to assess the opposition. Yet destiny has arranged that this strategic observation will transform into an existential crisis when he recognizes his own relatives among the enemy ranks.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
योत्स्यमानानवेक्षेऽहं य एतेऽत्र समागताः।
धार्तराष्ट्रस्य दुर्बुद्धेर्युद्धे प्रियचिकीर्षवः॥२३॥
verses.transliteration:
Yotsyamanan-Avekshe'ham Ya Ete'tra Samagatah.
Dhartarashtrasya Durbuddher-Yuddhe Priya-Chikirsavah.||23||
verses.hindiTranslation: मैं देखना चाहता हूँ कि दुर्बुद्धि दुर्योधन का युद्ध में प्रिय करने की इच्छा से यहाँ कौन-कौन योद्धा एकत्र हुए हैं।
verses.englishTranslation: Let me see those who have come here to fight, wishing to please the evil-minded son of Dhritarashtra in battle.
verses.commentary: Arjuna calls Duryodhana 'durbuddhi' (evil-minded), showing his moral clarity about who is wrong. Ironically, despite knowing Duryodhana's wickedness, Arjuna will soon be paralyzed by compassion for the very warriors who support this evil cause.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.devanagari:
एवमुक्तो हृषीकेशो गुडाकेशेन भारत।
सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये स्थापयित्वा रथोत्तमम्॥२४॥
verses.transliteration:
Evam-Ukto Hrishikesho Gudakeshena Bharata.
Senayor-Ubhayor-Madhye Sthapayitva Rathottamam.||24||
verses.hindiTranslation: संजय ने कहा — हे भारत! अर्जुन (गुडाकेश) के ऐसा कहने पर श्रीकृष्ण (हृषीकेश) ने उत्तम रथ को दोनों सेनाओं के बीच में खड़ा कर दिया।
verses.englishTranslation: Sanjaya said: O Bharata (Dhritarashtra), thus addressed by Gudakesha (Arjuna), Hrishikesha (Krishna) placed the finest chariot between the two armies.
verses.commentary: Krishna, the Supreme Lord, willingly becomes Arjuna's charioteer and obeys his command. The epithets are meaningful — Hrishikesha (lord of the senses) drives the chariot for Gudakesha (conqueror of sleep/ignorance). This symbolizes how the divine guides the sincere seeker.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.devanagari:
भीष्मद्रोणप्रमुखतः सर्वेषां च महीक्षिताम्।
उवाच पार्थ पश्यैतान्समवेतान्कुरूनिति॥२५॥
verses.transliteration:
Bhishma-Drona-Pramukhatah Sarvesham Cha Mahikshitam.
Uvacha Partha Pashyai-Tan Samavetan Kurun-Iti.||25||

wordByWord.meaning: in front of Bhishma and Drona

verses.hindiTranslation: भीष्म, द्रोण और समस्त राजाओं के सामने रथ को खड़ा करके श्रीकृष्ण ने कहा — हे पार्थ! इन एकत्रित कुरुवंशियों को देखो।
verses.englishTranslation: In the presence of Bhishma, Drona, and all the rulers of the world, Krishna said: O Partha, behold these assembled Kurus.
verses.commentary: Krishna deliberately places the chariot where Arjuna can see Bhishma and Drona most clearly — the two elders Arjuna reveres the most. Krishna's simple words 'behold these Kurus' carry deep irony, as what Arjuna sees will shatter his resolve to fight.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.devanagari:
तत्रापश्यत्स्थितान्पार्थः पितॄनथ पितामहान्।
आचार्यान्मातुलान्भ्रातॄन्पुत्रान्पौत्रान्सखींस्तथा॥२६॥
verses.transliteration:
Tatra-Apashyat Sthitan Parthah Pitrin-Atha Pitamahan.
Acharyan Matulan Bhratrin Putran Pautran Sakhin-Statha.||26||
verses.hindiTranslation: वहाँ अर्जुन ने दोनों सेनाओं में खड़े पितृतुल्य बड़ों, पितामहों, आचार्यों, मामाओं, भाइयों, पुत्रों, पौत्रों और मित्रों को देखा।
verses.englishTranslation: There Arjuna saw, standing in both armies, fathers, grandfathers, teachers, maternal uncles, brothers, sons, grandsons, and friends.
verses.commentary: This is the pivotal moment — Arjuna sees not enemies but family. Every relationship a person holds dear is represented here. The comprehensive list emphasizes that this war will tear apart the very fabric of family and society, which becomes the source of Arjuna's anguish.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.devanagari:
श्वशुरान्सुहृदश्चैव सेनयोरुभयोरपि।
तान्समीक्ष्य स कौन्तेयः सर्वान्बन्धूनवस्थितान्॥२७॥
verses.transliteration:
Shvashuran Suhridash-Chaiva Senayor-Ubhayor-Api.
Tan Samikshya Sa Kaunteyah Sarvan Bandhun-Avasthitan.||27||
verses.hindiTranslation: दोनों सेनाओं में उपस्थित श्वसुरों और सुहृदों (हितैषियों) को भी देखकर, कुन्तीपुत्र अर्जुन ने उन सब बन्धुओं को वहाँ खड़ा देखा।
verses.englishTranslation: He also saw fathers-in-law and well-wishers in both armies. Seeing all these relatives present there, the son of Kunti (Arjuna) was deeply moved.
verses.commentary: Verses 26-27 form a continuous description. The word 'ubhayoh' (both) is crucial — relatives are on both sides. This makes the war a family tragedy regardless of who wins. Arjuna's compassion arises from seeing the universality of kinship across enemy lines.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
कृपया परयाविष्टो विषीदन्निदमब्रवीत्।
दृष्ट्वेमं स्वजनं कृष्ण युयुत्सुं समुपस्थितम्॥२८॥
verses.transliteration:
Kripaya Paraya-Avishto Vishidann-Idam-Abravit.
Drishtve-Mam Svajanam Krishna Yuyutsum Samupasthitam.||28||
verses.hindiTranslation: अर्जुन ने कहा — हे कृष्ण! युद्ध के लिए उपस्थित इन अपने स्वजनों को देखकर मेरे अंग शिथिल हुए जा रहे हैं, गहन करुणा से मैं व्याकुल हूँ।
verses.englishTranslation: Arjuna said: O Krishna, seeing my own kinsmen gathered here eager to fight, I am overcome with deep compassion and feel distressed.
verses.commentary: This marks the beginning of Arjuna's despondency. The word 'kripa' (compassion) is significant — Arjuna's crisis is born not from cowardice but from a deeply compassionate heart. However, Krishna will later explain that this is 'misplaced' compassion rooted in attachment rather than true wisdom.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
सीदन्ति मम गात्राणि मुखं च परिशुष्यति।
वेपथुश्च शरीरे मे रोमहर्षश्च जायते॥२९॥
verses.transliteration:
Sidanti Mama Gatrani Mukham Cha Parishushyati.
Vepathush-Cha Sharire Me Roma-Harshash-Cha Jayate.||29||
verses.hindiTranslation: मेरे अंग शिथिल हो रहे हैं, मुख सूख रहा है, शरीर में कम्पन हो रहा है और रोंगटे खड़े हो रहे हैं।
verses.englishTranslation: My limbs are giving way, my mouth is drying up, my body is trembling, and my hair is standing on end.
verses.commentary: Arjuna describes classic symptoms of acute anxiety and emotional shock. These physical symptoms — weakness, dry mouth, trembling, and horripilation — are the body's natural response to extreme psychological distress. The greatest warrior of his time is rendered helpless by his own emotions.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
गाण्डीवं स्रंसते हस्तात्त्वक्चैव परिदह्यते।
न च शक्नोम्यवस्थातुं भ्रमतीव च मे मनः॥३०॥
verses.transliteration:
Gandivam Sransate Hastat-Tvak-Chaiva Paridahyate.
Na Cha Shaknomy-Avasthatum Bhramati-Iva Cha Me Manah.||30||
verses.hindiTranslation: गाण्डीव धनुष हाथ से गिर रहा है, त्वचा जल रही है, मैं खड़ा रहने में असमर्थ हूँ और मेरा मन भ्रमित हो रहा है।
verses.englishTranslation: The Gandiva bow is slipping from my hand, my skin is burning, I am unable to stand, and my mind seems to be whirling.
verses.commentary: The Gandiva bow, a divine weapon that had never failed Arjuna, is now slipping from his grasp. This is deeply symbolic — when the mind loses clarity, even the most powerful instruments become useless. The burning skin and spinning mind indicate the total collapse of his warrior composure.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
निमित्तानि च पश्यामि विपरीतानि केशव।
न च श्रेयोऽनुपश्यामि हत्वा स्वजनमाहवे॥३१॥
verses.transliteration:
Nimittani Cha Pashyami Viparitani Keshava.
Na Cha Shreyo'nupashyami Hatva Svajanam-Ahave.||31||
verses.hindiTranslation: हे केशव! मुझे विपरीत (अशुभ) लक्षण दिखाई दे रहे हैं और युद्ध में अपने स्वजनों को मारकर मुझे कोई कल्याण नहीं दिखता।
verses.englishTranslation: O Keshava, I see adverse omens, and I cannot foresee any good from killing my own kinsmen in battle.
verses.commentary: Arjuna now invokes inauspicious omens to justify his reluctance. While his compassion is genuine, Krishna will later reveal that Arjuna's reasoning is clouded by attachment. True welfare lies in fulfilling one's dharmic duty, not in avoiding difficult actions out of emotional confusion.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
न काङ्क्षे विजयं कृष्ण न च राज्यं सुखानि च।
किं नो राज्येन गोविन्द किं भोगैर्जीवितेन वा॥३२॥
verses.transliteration:
Na Kankshye Vijayam Krishna Na Cha Rajyam Sukhani Cha.
Kim No Rajyena Govinda Kim Bhogair-Jivitena Va.||32||
verses.hindiTranslation: हे कृष्ण! मैं न विजय चाहता हूँ, न राज्य और न सुख। हे गोविन्द! हमें राज्य से क्या लाभ? भोगों से या जीवन से भी क्या?
verses.englishTranslation: O Krishna, I do not desire victory, nor a kingdom, nor pleasures. O Govinda, of what use is a kingdom to us? What is the use of enjoyments or even life itself?
verses.commentary: Arjuna renounces the very goals of the war — victory, kingdom, and pleasure. This extreme renunciation born from despair looks noble but is actually a form of escapism. Krishna will teach that true renunciation is not abandoning action but abandoning attachment to results while performing one's duty.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
येषामर्थे काङ्क्षितं नो राज्यं भोगाः सुखानि च।
त इमेऽवस्थिता युद्धे प्राणांस्त्यक्त्वा धनानि च॥३३॥
verses.transliteration:
Yesham-Arthe Kankshitam No Rajyam Bhogah Sukhani Cha.
Ta Ime'vasthita Yuddhe Pranams-Tyaktva Dhanani Cha.||33||
verses.hindiTranslation: जिनके लिए हम राज्य, भोग और सुख चाहते हैं, वे ही ये सब लोग अपने प्राण और धन को त्यागकर युद्ध में खड़े हैं।
verses.englishTranslation: Those for whose sake we desire kingdom, enjoyments, and pleasures — they are standing here in battle, ready to give up their lives and wealth.
verses.commentary: Arjuna presents a powerful logical argument: the very people for whom one desires wealth and happiness are the ones who will perish in the war. What good is a kingdom enjoyed without the loved ones for whom it was sought? This is the tragedy of all wars — victory brings loss.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
आचार्याः पितरः पुत्रास्तथैव च पितामहाः।
मातुलाः श्वशुराः पौत्राः श्यालाः सम्बन्धिनस्तथा॥३४॥
verses.transliteration:
Acharyah Pitarah Putras-Tathaiva Cha Pitamahah.
Matulah Shvashurah Pautrah Shyalah Sambandhinas-Tatha.||34||
verses.hindiTranslation: गुरुजन, पिता, पुत्र, पितामह, मामा, श्वसुर, पौत्र, साले और अन्य सम्बन्धी — ये सब यहाँ हैं।
verses.englishTranslation: Teachers, fathers, sons, grandfathers, maternal uncles, fathers-in-law, grandsons, brothers-in-law, and other relatives — they are all here.
verses.commentary: Arjuna meticulously lists every category of family relationship to emphasize that this is not merely a war between kings but a devastating family conflict. Each relationship category represents a sacred bond in Indian culture that will be destroyed by the violence of war.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
एतान्न हन्तुमिच्छामि घ्नतोऽपि मधुसूदन।
अपि त्रैलोक्यराज्यस्य हेतोः किं नु महीकृते॥३५॥
verses.transliteration:
Etan Na Hantum-Ichchhami Ghnato'pi Madhusudana.
Api Trailokya-Rajyasya Hetoh Kim Nu Mahikrite.||35||

wordByWord.meaning: of the sovereignty of three worlds
for the sake of the earth

verses.hindiTranslation: हे मधुसूदन! ये सब मुझे मार भी डालें तो भी मैं इन्हें मारना नहीं चाहता। तीनों लोकों के राज्य के लिए भी नहीं, फिर इस पृथ्वी के लिए तो कहना ही क्या?
verses.englishTranslation: O Madhusudana, I do not wish to kill them even if they kill me — not even for the sovereignty of all three worlds, let alone for this earth.
verses.commentary: Arjuna declares his willingness to die rather than fight. He offers the ultimate sacrifice — not just of victory but of life itself. While this seems supremely noble, Krishna will show that abandoning one's dharmic duty out of attachment is not true sacrifice but rather spiritual confusion.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
निहत्य धार्तराष्ट्रान्नः का प्रीतिः स्याज्जनार्दन।
पापमेवाश्रयेदस्मान्हत्वैतानाततायिनः॥३६॥
verses.transliteration:
Nihatya Dhartarashtran Nah Ka Pritih Syaj-Janardana.
Papam-Eva-Ashrayed-Asman Hatva-Etan-Atatayinah.||36||
verses.hindiTranslation: हे जनार्दन! धृतराष्ट्र के पुत्रों को मारकर हमें क्या प्रसन्नता होगी? इन आततायियों को मारने से तो हमें पाप ही लगेगा।
verses.englishTranslation: O Janardana, what pleasure would we derive from killing the sons of Dhritarashtra? Sin alone would accrue to us by slaying these aggressors.
verses.commentary: Interestingly, Arjuna acknowledges the Kauravas are 'atatayinah' (aggressors) — yet still refuses to fight them. According to dharma-shastra, killing an aggressor (one who commits arson, poisoning, armed attack, theft, land-grabbing, or abduction) incurs no sin. Arjuna's emotional state overrides his scriptural knowledge.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
तस्मान्नार्हा वयं हन्तुं धार्तराष्ट्रान्स्वबान्धवान्।
स्वजनं हि कथं हत्वा सुखिनः स्याम माधव॥३७॥
verses.transliteration:
Tasman-Narha Vayam Hantum Dhartarashtran Sva-Bandhavan.
Svajanam Hi Katham Hatva Sukhinah Syama Madhava.||37||
verses.hindiTranslation: इसलिए हमें अपने बन्धु धार्तराष्ट्रों को मारना उचित नहीं है। हे माधव! अपने स्वजनों को मारकर हम कैसे सुखी हो सकते हैं?
verses.englishTranslation: Therefore, it is not right for us to kill the sons of Dhritarashtra, our own relatives. O Madhava, how can we be happy by killing our own kinsmen?
verses.commentary: Arjuna's argument seems logical on the surface — happiness cannot come from killing family. Yet this reasoning confuses temporary emotional suffering with lasting dharmic consequences. Krishna will later explain that greater harm comes from abandoning one's righteous duty than from the pain of fulfilling it.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
यद्यप्येते न पश्यन्ति लोभोपहतचेतसः।
कुलक्षयकृतं दोषं मित्रद्रोहे च पातकम्॥३८॥
verses.transliteration:
Yady-Apy-Ete Na Pashyanti Lobho-Pahata-Chetasah.
Kula-Kshaya-Kritam Dosham Mitra-Drohe Cha Patakam.||38||
verses.hindiTranslation: यद्यपि लोभ से भ्रष्ट बुद्धि वाले ये लोग कुल के नाश से उत्पन्न दोष और मित्रद्रोह में पाप नहीं देखते,
verses.englishTranslation: Even though these people, whose minds are overcome by greed, see no wrong in destroying the family or sin in betraying friends,
verses.commentary: Arjuna recognizes that the Kauravas are blinded by greed, yet he takes the moral high ground by refusing to descend to their level. This verse begins a philosophical argument spanning several verses about the consequences of family destruction — showing Arjuna's intellect trying to rationalize his emotional paralysis.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
कथं न ज्ञेयमस्माभिः पापादस्मान्निवर्तितुम्।
कुलक्षयकृतं दोषं प्रपश्यद्भिर्जनार्दन॥३९॥
verses.transliteration:
Katham Na Jneyam-Asmabhih Papad-Asman Nivartitum.
Kula-Kshaya-Kritam Dosham Prapashyadbhir-Janardana.||39||
verses.hindiTranslation: हे जनार्दन! परन्तु कुल-नाश के दोष को जानने वाले हमें इस पाप से हटने का विवेक क्यों नहीं होना चाहिए?
verses.englishTranslation: O Janardana, why should we, who can clearly see the evil in destroying the family, not have the wisdom to turn away from this sin?
verses.commentary: Arjuna claims moral superiority — if the Kauravas are blind to sin, those who can see it (like the Pandavas) bear even greater responsibility to avoid it. This argument sounds wise but confuses passive avoidance of sin with active performance of duty, a distinction Krishna will clarify.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
कुलक्षये प्रणश्यन्ति कुलधर्माः सनातनाः।
धर्मे नष्टे कुलं कृत्स्नमधर्मोऽभिभवत्युत॥४०॥
verses.transliteration:
Kula-Kshaye Pranashyanti Kula-Dharmah Sanatanah.
Dharme Nashte Kulam Kritsnam-Adharmo'bhibhavaty-Uta.||40||

wordByWord.meaning: in the destruction of the family

verses.hindiTranslation: कुल के नाश होने पर सनातन कुलधर्म नष्ट हो जाते हैं। धर्म के नष्ट होने पर सम्पूर्ण कुल को अधर्म दबा लेता है।
verses.englishTranslation: When the family is destroyed, the ancient family traditions perish. When dharma is lost, adharma (irreligion) overwhelms the entire family.
verses.commentary: Arjuna articulates a legitimate social concern — the destruction of elders leads to the loss of cultural and spiritual traditions passed down through generations. Without these guardians of tradition, moral decay follows. This sociological argument shows the depth of Arjuna's thinking beyond personal grief.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
अधर्माभिभवात्कृष्ण प्रदुष्यन्ति कुलस्त्रियः।
स्त्रीषु दुष्टासु वार्ष्णेय जायते वर्णसङ्करः॥४१॥
verses.transliteration:
Adharma-Abhibhavat Krishna Pradushyanti Kula-Striyah.
Strishu Dushtasu Varshneya Jayate Varna-Sankarah.||41||
verses.hindiTranslation: हे कृष्ण! अधर्म के बढ़ने से कुल की स्त्रियाँ दूषित हो जाती हैं। हे वार्ष्णेय! स्त्रियों के दूषित होने पर वर्णसंकर (सामाजिक अव्यवस्था) उत्पन्न होता है।
verses.englishTranslation: O Krishna, with the prevalence of adharma, the women of the family become corrupted. O Varshneya, when women are corrupted, social disorder (varna-sankara) arises.
verses.commentary: Arjuna speaks from the perspective of his era's social framework. The deeper point is about the breakdown of social order when moral structures collapse — a universal truth applicable to any society. When the guardians of values are destroyed in war, the entire social fabric unravels.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
सङ्करो नरकायैव कुलघ्नानां कुलस्य च।
पतन्ति पितरो ह्येषां लुप्तपिण्डोदकक्रियाः॥४२॥
verses.transliteration:
Sankaro Narakayaiva Kula-Ghnanam Kulasya Cha.
Patanti Pitaro Hy-Esham Lupta-Pindodaka-Kriyah.||42||

wordByWord.meaning: of the destroyers of the family
deprived of offerings of food and water

verses.hindiTranslation: यह वर्णसंकर कुल-घातियों और कुल दोनों को नरक में ले जाता है। इनके पितर भी पिण्ड और जल की क्रिया बन्द होने से गिर जाते हैं।
verses.englishTranslation: Such social disorder leads to hell for both the destroyers of the family and the family itself. The ancestors fall, deprived of the offerings of food and water.
verses.commentary: Arjuna invokes the ancient Vedic belief in ancestral rites (shraddha). When families are destroyed, the ritual offerings to ancestors cease, causing their spiritual downfall. This concern for both the living and the departed shows the comprehensive nature of Arjuna's moral anguish.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
दोषैरेतैः कुलघ्नानां वर्णसङ्करकारकैः।
उत्साद्यन्ते जातिधर्माः कुलधर्माश्च शाश्वताः॥४३॥
verses.transliteration:
Doshair-Etaih Kula-Ghnanam Varna-Sankara-Karakaih.
Utsadyante Jati-Dharmah Kula-Dharmash-Cha Shashvatah.||43||
verses.hindiTranslation: इन वर्णसंकर उत्पन्न करने वाले दोषों से कुल-घातियों के सनातन जाति-धर्म और कुल-धर्म नष्ट हो जाते हैं।
verses.englishTranslation: By the misdeeds of those who destroy the family and create social disorder, the eternal community traditions and family dharma are ruined.
verses.commentary: Arjuna's concern extends beyond individuals to the entire social structure. The destruction of 'shashvata' (eternal) traditions means the loss of accumulated spiritual wisdom passed through generations. This argument, while valid in context, represents attachment to social order over cosmic order — a distinction Krishna will address.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
उत्सन्नकुलधर्माणां मनुष्याणां जनार्दन।
नरकेऽनियतं वासो भवतीत्यनुशुश्रुम॥४४॥
verses.transliteration:
Utsanna-Kula-Dharmanam Manushyanam Janardana.
Narake'niyatam Vaso Bhavati-Ity-Anushushruma.||44||

wordByWord.meaning: of those whose family traditions are destroyed

verses.hindiTranslation: हे जनार्दन! हमने सुना है कि जिन मनुष्यों के कुल-धर्म नष्ट हो जाते हैं, उन्हें अनिश्चित काल तक नरक में वास करना पड़ता है।
verses.englishTranslation: O Janardana, we have heard from the learned that those whose family traditions are destroyed are destined to dwell in hell for an indefinite period.
verses.commentary: Arjuna cites scriptural authority ('anushushruma' — we have heard from the wise) to strengthen his argument. He invokes the fear of hell as the ultimate consequence. Ironically, it is precisely this kind of scriptural half-knowledge that Krishna will correct with complete spiritual wisdom.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
अहो बत महत्पापं कर्तुं व्यवसिता वयम्।
यद्राज्यसुखलोभेन हन्तुं स्वजनमुद्यताः॥४५॥
verses.transliteration:
Aho Bata Mahat-Papam Kartum Vyavasita Vayam.
Yad-Rajya-Sukha-Lobhena Hantum Svajanam-Udyatah.||45||

wordByWord.meaning: by greed for kingdom and pleasure

verses.hindiTranslation: हाय! कितने खेद की बात है कि हम लोग बड़ा भारी पाप करने को तैयार हो गए हैं — राज्य और सुख के लोभ से अपने स्वजनों को मारने पर उतारू हैं।
verses.englishTranslation: Alas! How strange it is that we are resolved to commit a great sin, driven by the greed for kingdom and pleasure, prepared to kill our own kinsmen.
verses.commentary: The exclamation 'Aho bata' expresses Arjuna's deep anguish and self-reproach. He now includes himself in the blame — 'we' have resolved to commit sin. This self-awareness, while showing moral sensitivity, has paralyzed him into inaction, which itself becomes a failure of duty.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
यदि मामप्रतीकारमशस्त्रं शस्त्रपाणयः।
धार्तराष्ट्रा रणे हन्युस्तन्मे क्षेमतरं भवेत्॥४६॥
verses.transliteration:
Yadi Mam-Apratikaram-Ashastram Shastra-Panayah.
Dhartarashtra Rane Hanyus-Tan-Me Kshemataram Bhavet.||46||
verses.hindiTranslation: यदि शस्त्र हाथ में लिए हुए धृतराष्ट्र के पुत्र युद्ध में मुझ निहत्थे और प्रतिकार न करने वाले को मार दें, तो वह मेरे लिए अधिक कल्याणकारी होगा।
verses.englishTranslation: If the sons of Dhritarashtra, weapons in hand, were to kill me in battle — unarmed and unresisting — that would be better for me.
verses.commentary: This is the climax of Arjuna's despair. He would rather die unarmed than fight. For a Kshatriya, this is the ultimate renunciation — choosing death over the warrior's duty. This extreme position reveals how completely Arjuna's emotional crisis has overwhelmed his sense of dharma.

verses.speaker: Sanjaya
verses.devanagari:
एवमुक्त्वार्जुनः सङ्ख्ये रथोपस्थ उपाविशत्।
विसृज्य सशरं चापं शोकसंविग्नमानसः॥४७॥
verses.transliteration:
Evam-Uktva-Arjunah Sankhye Rathopastha Upavishat.
Visrijya Sa-Sharam Chapam Shoka-Samvigna-Manasah.||47||

wordByWord.meaning: on the seat of the chariot
with a mind agitated by grief

verses.hindiTranslation: संजय ने कहा — ऐसा कहकर अर्जुन रणभूमि में शोक से व्याकुल मन वाले होकर बाण सहित धनुष को छोड़कर रथ के पिछले भाग में बैठ गए।
verses.englishTranslation: Sanjaya said: Having spoken thus on the battlefield, Arjuna cast aside his bow and arrows and sank into the seat of his chariot, his mind overwhelmed with grief.
verses.commentary: The chapter ends with this powerful image — the greatest archer of his era sitting down in defeat before the battle has even begun, his weapons abandoned. This moment of complete psychological collapse is the necessary darkness before the dawn of Krishna's teachings. Without this depth of despair, Arjuna would not have been ready to receive the highest wisdom.
